figures.gloss: The absolute, supreme creator who forged the cosmos and then withdrew, delegating its administration to the Heptad. Xwedê is entirely ineffable, wholly good, and removed from the direct, daily governance of human affairs.
figures.facet: Lacks any opposing cosmic adversary. Yazidism fundamentally rejects dualistic evil; because Xwedê is omnipotent and perfectly good, a sovereign entity of evil (like Satan) cannot logically exist in this cosmology.
figures.provenance: Oral Qewls (sacred hymns); Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism: Its Background, Observances and Textual Tradition.'

figures.gloss: The chief of the Seven Holy Beings and the active executor of the cosmos. When Xwedê commanded the angels to bow to newly created Adam, Tawûsê Melek refused, honoring a prior command to bow to none but God.
figures.facet: His refusal was a triumphant test of strict monotheistic loyalty, not a rebellion. He is a perfectly devoted servant whose tears of remorse for any perceived friction extinguished the fires of Hell, rendering Abrahamic 'fallen angel' accusations tragic misunderstandings.
figures.provenance: Oral Qewls (the tradition's authentic scriptural core); the Mishefa Reş and Kitêba Cilwe, early-20th-century compilations whose antiquity and authenticity scholars including Kreyenbroek regard as doubtful.

figures.gloss: A 12th-century Sufi mystic who reformed and codified the Yazidi faith, establishing its strict caste system. He is revered by the community not merely as a mortal teacher, but as an earthly manifestation or avatar of Tawûsê Melek.
figures.facet: He represents the ultimate survival synthesis: adopting the outward vocabulary and aesthetic of esoteric Sufi Islam to encrypt and preserve a profoundly non-Islamic, indigenous Kurdish cosmology against centuries of persecution.
figures.provenance: Yazidi oral tradition; historical Islamic chronicles.

figures.gloss: The primordial vessel of creation formed from God's own pure essence. It rested for forty thousand years upon a cosmic bird before shattering; its fragmented pieces formed the heavens, the earth, and the celestial bodies.
figures.facet: It renders the material universe profoundly sacred. Creation is not an ex nihilo spoken command, but the literal, physical shattering and unfolding of God's own concentrated light, making nature intrinsically divine.
figures.provenance: Oral Qewlê Afirandinê (Qewls of Creation), the tradition's authentic scriptural core; the Mishefa Reş, an early-20th-century compilation of doubtful authenticity per Kreyenbroek and others.

figures.gloss: The seven divine emanations of God's light, corresponding to the days of the week. Led by Tawûsê Melek, they are the immediate stewards of reality, governing the forces of nature and the destiny of humanity.
figures.facet: They dynamically bridge heaven and earth by periodically incarnating as human holy men (such as the historical companions of Sheikh Adi) to actively guide and realign the Yazidi community.

figures.gloss: The holiest valley in Kurdistan and the spiritual center of the Yazidi world, housing the tomb of Sheikh Adi. It is the geographic and cosmic pivot point where the heavenly and earthly realms intersect.
figures.facet: It is cosmologically remembered as the first solid land to descend from heaven, acting as a divine peg to stabilize the chaotic, shifting waters of the newly formed earth.

figures.gloss: The transmigration of the soul. At death, the soul discards its worn physical body like an old shirt and immediately dons a new one, embarking on a continuous cycle of purification and rebirth.
figures.facet: Unlike expansive karmic systems, this cycle is tightly ethnoreligious. A Yazidi soul reincarnates exclusively as a Yazidi, preserving the spiritual lineage of the community until ultimate purification allows return to the divine light.

figures.gloss: The solar manifestation among the Seven Holy Beings. Yazidis face the sun during their prayers, venerating it as the visible, purest emanation of God's light and the ultimate witness to all earthly oaths.
figures.facet: The sun is worshipped not as an independent creator god, but as an incorruptible 'Qibla' (direction of prayer)—a physical proxy for the divine presence that cannot be polluted by human darkness.
figures.provenance: Daily morning prayers (Dua Sibê); Yazidi oral tradition.

figures.gloss: A divine being of light, third member of the central holy trinity alongside Tawûsê Melek and Şêx Adî, and the eponym in whose name the Êzidî community is named. Scholars (Kreyenbroek) trace the name historically to the Umayyad caliph Yazid ibn Mu'awiya, but within Yazidi theology he is wholly transfigured into a divine emanation unconnected to that historical figure.
figures.facet: Sultan Êzî shows how a communal eponym can be a documented historical borrowing and a fully sacralized, unrelated theological person at once — the tradition's own name for itself encodes the transfiguration.
figures.provenance: P.G. Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism: Its Background, Observances and Textual Tradition'; Yazidi oral Qewls.

figures.gloss: Sheikh Hasan ibn Adi, successor in Şêx Adî's line and venerated as the incarnation of one of the Heft Sirr; associated with the pen and the mystery of writing, and the ancestor-figure of the Şemsanî/Adanî sheikhly division.
figures.facet: Ties the Heft Sirr's abstract stewardship of reality to a concrete, named human lineage of religious authority that still structures caste divisions among Yazidi sheikhs today.
figures.provenance: Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism'; J.S. Guest, 'Survival Among the Kurds: A History of the Yezidis.'

figures.gloss: One of the Heft Sirr, associated with the moon as Şêx Şems is associated with the sun; like Şêx Şems, he is both an angelic being and the ancestor-figure of a Yazidi sheikhly lineage.
figures.facet: Completes a sun/moon pairing within the Heft Sirr, showing the heptad's stewardship of the world organized in part around paired celestial complements rather than seven isolated offices.

figures.gloss: The corpus of orally transmitted sacred hymns, composed in a specialized register of Kurmanji and preserved and performed by hereditary qewwals; the tradition's actual functioning scripture, only sporadically committed to writing by scholars and community members in the 20th–21st centuries.
figures.facet: Unlike the Mishefa Reş and Kitêba Cilwe, which scholars regard as early-20th-century compilations of doubtful antiquity, the Qewls are the genuinely old and living scriptural core — a canon that resists textualization by design.
figures.provenance: Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism: Its Background, Observances and Textual Tradition' (the foundational study of the Qewl corpus); Kreyenbroek & Rashow, 'God and Sheikh Adi are Perfect.'

figures.gloss: The Yazidi New Year festival, held on the first Wednesday of April by the Julian calendar, marking the day Tawûsê Melek first descended to Lalish and the earth congealed into solid form. Celebrants dye eggs and gather red flowers, especially anemones.
figures.facet: Anchors the tradition's creation narrative to a fixed, still-observed calendar date, turning cosmology into a lived annual festival rather than only a narrated myth.
figures.provenance: Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism'; C. Allison, 'The Yezidi Oral Tradition in Iraqi Kurdistan.'

figures.gloss: The seven-day autumn pilgrimage-festival at Lalish, the tradition's central collective rite, at which the community gathers to honor the Heft Sirr and a bull is ritually sacrificed at the shrine of Şêx Şems.
figures.facet: Where reverence for the Seven is elsewhere abstract doctrine, Cejna Cemaiya renders it an annual, physically embodied mass pilgrimage binding the scattered community back to a single sacred valley.
figures.provenance: Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism'; Allison, 'The Yezidi Oral Tradition in Iraqi Kurdistan.'

figures.gloss: Seven consecrated bronze peacock effigies representing Tawûsê Melek, historically carried in circuit (tawûsgerran) among Yazidi settlements by custodian qewwals, received with prayer, offerings, and the collection of religious dues.
figures.facet: Distributes a single angelic presence into seven physical, portable vessels, letting scattered Yazidi communities each stand before Tawûsê Melek in turn rather than only at the shrine of Lalish.

edges.note: Xwedê is the passive, unknowable origin, while Tawûsê Melek is the active, executive force of the cosmos.
Sheikh Adi is deeply revered as the physical, earthly incarnation of the Peacock Angel.
Lalish was the first piece of the shattered Pearl to solidify, anchoring the rest of the earth.
GOLD SEAM: Xwedê shares linguistic and regional history with the Islamic Allah, representing absolute, unpartnered monotheism.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent an entirely unknowable, infinite divine reality that only interacts with creation through a series of structured emanations.
GOLD SEAM: Connects to the Iranian supreme creator of unalloyed good, devoid of any inherent darkness.
GOLD SEAM: Tawûsê Melek is the ultimate inversion of the Zoroastrian dark spirit—accused of being the adversary by outsiders, but structurally representing pure loyalty and the extinguishing of hell.
GOLD SEAM: Both are eternally youthful, martial emanations intrinsically symbolized by the peacock, serving as the active guides of their devotees.
GOLD SEAM: A divine figure who undertakes an act widely misinterpreted as rebellion, but which ultimately positions him as the active champion and caretaker of the earthly realm.
GOLD SEAM: Charismatic Near Eastern reformers who codified entirely new faith structures by syncretizing older, indigenous cosmologies with Abrahamic aesthetics.
GOLD SEAM: The primordial, unbroken vessel from which the heavens, earth, and celestial bodies are forged when it physically shatters.
GOLD SEAM: The initial, unmanifested shape that holds the divine blueprint of the cosmos before breaking open to form material reality.
GOLD SEAM: A fundamental heptad of divine beings emanating directly from the supreme God, tasked with maintaining the physical and spiritual order of the universe.
GOLD SEAM: The central cosmic mountain or peak that acts as the geographic and spiritual anchor of the physical world.
GOLD SEAM: The central vertical axis that stabilizes the cosmos and connects the heavenly, earthly, and subterranean realms.
GOLD SEAM: A continuous cycle of rebirth that the soul must navigate until it achieves sufficient purification.
GOLD SEAM: A tightly closed loop of reincarnation where souls are immediately reborn directly within their specific kin or ethnoreligious community.
GOLD SEAM: The central Near Eastern figure of solar light, overseeing oaths, purity, and the visible manifestation of divine truth.
GOLD SEAM: The sun as a pure, divine eye and the crucial focal point for daily orienting prayers.
Intra-tradition: two-thirds of the central holy trinity, together with Şêx Adî.
Intra-tradition: completing the trinity of Tawûsê Melek, Şêx Adî, and Sultan Êzî.
Intra-tradition: a named member of the Seven Holy Beings, incarnate in Şêx Adî's line.
Intra-tradition: successor in the Şêx Adî lineage, founder of the Şemsanî/Adanî division.
Intra-tradition: the moon and sun complements within the Heft Sirr.
Intra-tradition: the oral corpus in which the Seven's doctrine is preserved and transmitted.
Intra-tradition: the New Year festival commemorating his first descent to Lalish.
Intra-tradition: commemorates the day the shattered White Pearl's fragments solidified into earth.
Intra-tradition: the central collective pilgrimage-rite performed at the sacred sanctuary.
Intra-tradition: the annual feast at which the Seven are collectively honored.
Intra-tradition: the consecrated cultic vessels of the Peacock Angel's physical presence.
two closely related Kurdish esoteric traditions each anchor authority in a jealously guarded, largely oral scriptural corpus rather than a public written canon.

Same narrative referent — the angel commanded to bow to Adam who refused — with opposite valuation: Islam reads the refusal as damnation (Iblis), Yazidism as the supreme proof of monotheist loyalty; the equation is external polemic Yazidism itself rejects, denying any Satan exists in its theology.

dossier.essence: A monotheistic, endogamous religion of the Kurdish-speaking Yazidis (Ezidi), historically centered on the shrine valley of Lalish in northern Iraq, with communities in Syria, Turkey, the Caucasus, and a large recent diaspora. It venerates a withdrawn supreme God (Xwede) who entrusted the world to seven Holy Beings led by Tawuse Melek, the Peacock Angel. The faith was reformed in the 12th century around the Sufi Sheikh Adi ibn Musafir, and the community has suffered repeated persecution, culminating in the 2014 genocide by the Islamic State.
dossier.cosmos: God created the cosmos and delegated its governance to the Heptad (Heft Sirr), seven angelic emanations of his own light headed by Tawuse Melek, who, in the Yazidi telling, refused God's command to bow to Adam out of devotion to God alone and is a wholly good figure, not a fallen or evil one. Creation began when the primordial White Pearl (Durra Spi) shattered to form the world, with Lalish the first land to solidify. Souls transmigrate, 'changing the garment' (kiras guhartin), reborn within the community until purified and returned to the divine light.
dossier.practice: Society is strictly organized into endogamous castes, Sheikhs, Pirs, and Murids (laity), with religious knowledge held by initiated priestly lineages; marriage outside the community and conversion into it are traditionally impossible, and each layperson has an assigned sheikh, pir, and 'brother/sister of the hereafter.' Devotion includes daily prayer facing the sun (venerated as Sheikh Shems, a proxy for divine light), pilgrimage to Lalish and the autumn Feast of the Assembly (Cejna Cemaiya), seasonal fasts, the spring New Year (Carsema Sor, 'Red Wednesday'), and dietary and purity taboos.
dossier.canon: An oral tradition with no single authoritative book, carried in sacred hymns (qewls) and prayers recited by trained qewwals; the two texts sometimes cited as scriptures, the Mishefa Res ('Black Book') and Kiteba Cilwe ('Book of Revelation'), are of contested authenticity and likely late or outsider compilations.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds fidelity guarded by boundary, a self-enclosed community that preserves an ancient theology by refusing conversion and intermarriage, and that reads the 'rebel angel' of its neighbors as the supreme servant of God.
sources: Philip G. Kreyenbroek, 'Yezidism: Its Background, Observances and Textual Tradition' (1995)
Christine Allison, 'The Yezidi Oral Tradition in Iraqi Kurdistan' (2001)
